Page 1

BEARING 315
The Newsletter of the Cascade Orienteering Club
March-April 1990

Quest for the best, here in the Northwest

Plans are moving along for this August's North American 7-Day Competition. The map for our part in the extravaganza, the Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival and World Cup meet, has been field-checked and is being drafted at this very moment, thanks to good coordination by Fred Veler. The Finish Crew, headed by Jean Davis, worked out some of the rough spots at the Washington State Interscholastic Championships at St. Edward State Park in February. Sandy and Robert Miller are working hard on the PNWOF entry form, which will appear in next month's "Bearing 315." And Bruce McAlister and Kent Verbeck have arranged a lodging package at Central Washington University in Ellensburg.
This could be the opportunity of a lifetime. World-class orienteers running virtually in our backyard! Don't let the chance pass you by. Enter the Quest for the Best, here in the Northwest.

Pot-O-Luck '90

Rick and Eileen Breseman will kick off this year's Pot-O-Luck series with a potluck at their home on Thursday, April 19 at 6:30 p.m. Bring your favorite dish to 9528 Densmore Ave. N. and get to know other club members.

Do you know where you are?

That's what the University of Washington Psychology Department wants to know. More specifically, they want to know what makes orienteers more spacially aware than the rest of the population. In an effort to get some answers, a team of psychologists, headed by Susan Infield, has been conducting tests on orienteers and comparing the results to those of the general population. So far, orienteers have proven to be significantly better than the general population at knowing where they are in relation to their surroundings.
Future plans include testing orienteers at the Hamilton Corrals meet near Kamloops in May, and testing U.S. Team members at the Olympic Training Center in Colorado Springs.

Entry deadline for APOC extended!

The deadline to receive reduced rates for the APOC Festival has been extended to June 1. If you want to enter PNWOF only, you will receive an entry in the next issue of "Bearing 315" (yes, we said that last issue, but this time we really mean it). To receive an entry form for the entire 7 days of competition, send a stamped, self-addressed 8½ x 11 envelope to:
APOC entry
Cascade Orienteering Club
P.O. Box 31375
Seattle, WA 98103

Site for 1991 Washington State Summer Games chosen

Last month, officials of the Washington State Games Foundation announced that the 1991 Summer Games will be hosted by the cities of Bellevue, Kirkland, and Redmond. At the first Summer Games in Spokane last year, orienteering was very successful. Next year can be even more successful, so start thinking now about where to hold the orienteering competition.

Club Meeting March 12

Our next club meeting will be on Monday, March 12 at 7:00 p.m. at the University Branch Library (50th and Roosevelt) in Seattle. It will feature another installment of "Orienteering Tips for Beginners," and probably some other interesting discussions. Come find out for yourself

Governor Proclaims April Orienteering Month

Governor Booth Gardner signed a document officially proclaiming April "Orienteering Month" in the state of Washington. As you can see from the schedule on page 7, April is chock full of orienteering events. Try to fit one into your schedule, or attend them all!

Newcastle Island "A" meet

This year's Newcastle Island Meet is an "A" meet sanctioned by the Canadian Orienteering Federation. It is also the third race of this year's Island Series (other Series races: Thetis Lake, March 10/11; Maple Mountain, April 21/22; and Mount Douglas, October 13/14).
The meet will also be the Canadian half of this year's PIG WAR! The American meet will be two weeks later, June 9/10, on Orcas Island.
The two-day meet will feature 8 courses each day set to COF standards by Jim Traynor. Those who attended last year's Newcastle Meet wil remember Jim's interesting and challenging courses.
This promises to be the big meet of the B.C. spring orienteering season - just what you need to test yourself before the Asia-Pacific Championships.
For more information, call Kris Battistuzzi at (604) 743-9678.

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 17 February 1990. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Don Atkinson family, Betty Berreth, Elliot Biggerstaff, Margie Bone family, Boy Scout Troop 304, Breseman family, Paul Bullard, Don Cannon, Anna Coles, Ardis Dull, Len Englund, Brian Hart, Li-Chuan Huang, John Huth, Kent Jameson, Norm & Gerri King, Ken Lew, Bob & Suzy Lynch, Jim McNamara, Carl Moore, Debbie Newell, Michael Odom family, Sherrill Pattee, Craig Rose family, Mike Schuh, Roger Sharp, Robert Sunderland.

If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you want to get your handsome membership card, contact the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade sponsored meet or at one of the above-mentioned happenings. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs' functions. If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your newsletter to be late.

Now on to the fun stuff...Rick & Eileen Breseman's first child, Ryan, was born in January. Pat Albright and Peter Zurcher (Rocky Mountain O' Club) plan to marry in July and Pat will be moving to Colorado temporarily. If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column since I have such a bad memory.

Quest for the best, here in the Northwest

Plans are moving along for this August's North American 7-Day Competition. The map for our part in the extravaganza, the Pacific Northwest Orienteering Festival and World Cup meet, has been field-checked and is being drafted at this very moment, thanks to good coordination by Fred Veler. The Finish Crew, headed by Jean Davis, worked out some of the rough spots at the Washington State Interscholastic Championships at St. Edward State Park in February. Sandy and Robert Miller are working hard on the PNWOF entry form, which will appear in next month's "Bearing 315." And Bruce McAlister and Kent Verbeck have arranged a lodging package at Central Washington University in Ellensburg. This could be the opportunity of a lifetime. World-class orienteers running virtually in our backyard! Don't let the chance pass you by. Enter the Quest for the Best, here in the Northwest.

Rick and Eileen Breseman will kick off this year's Pot-O-Luck series with a potluck at their home on Thursday, April 19 at 6:30 p.m. Bring your favorite dish to 9528 Densmore Ave. N. and get to know other club members.
Do you know where you are?

That's what the University of Washington Psychology Department wants to know. More specifically, they want to know what makes orienteers more spacially aware than the rest of the population. In an effort to get some answers, a team of psychologists, headed by Susan Infield, has been conducting tests on orienteers and comparing the results to those of the general population. So far, orienteers have proven to be significantly better than the general population at knowing where they are in relation to their surroundings. Future plans include testing orienteers at the Hamilton Corrals meet near Kamloops in May, and testing U.S. Team members at the Olympic Training Center in Colorado Springs.

The deadline to receive reduced rates for the APOC Festival has been extended to June 1. If you want to enter PNWOF only, you will receive an entry in the next issue of "Bearing 315" (yes, we said that last issue, but this time we really mean it). To receive an entry form for the entire 7 days of competition, send a stamped, self-addressed 8½ x 11 envelope to: APOC entry Cascade Orienteering Club P.O. Box 31375 Seattle, WA 98103
Site for 1991 Washington State Summer Games chosen

Last month, officials of the Washington State Games Foundation announced that the 1991 Summer Games will be hosted by the cities of Bellevue, Kirkland, and Redmond. At the first Summer Games in Spokane last year, orienteering was very successful. Next year can be even more successful, so start thinking now about where to hold the orienteering competition.

Our next club meeting will be on Monday, March 12 at 7:00 p.m. at the University Branch Library (50th and Roosevelt) in Seattle. It will feature another installment of "Orienteering Tips for Beginners," and probably some other interesting discussions. Come find out for yourself

Governor Booth Gardner signed a document officially proclaiming April "Orienteering Month" in the state of Washington. As you can see from the schedule on page 7, April is chock full of orienteering events. Try to fit one into your schedule, or attend them all!

This year's Newcastle Island Meet is an "A" meet sanctioned by the Canadian Orienteering Federation. It is also the third race of this year's Island Series (other Series races: Thetis Lake, March 10/11; Maple Mountain, April 21/22; and Mount Douglas, October 13/14). The meet will also be the Canadian half of this year's PIG WAR! The American meet will be two weeks later, June 9/10, on Orcas Island. The two-day meet will feature 8 courses each day set to COF standards by Jim Traynor. Those who attended last year's Newcastle Meet wil remember Jim's interesting and challenging courses. This promises to be the big meet of the B.C. spring orienteering season - just what you need to test yourself before the Asia-Pacific Championships. For more information, call Kris Battistuzzi at (604) 743-9678.

As usual, we'll start with positive roster changes as of 17 February 1990. Words of both thanks and welcome go to the following new or renewing club members: Don Atkinson family, Betty Berreth, Elliot Biggerstaff, Margie Bone family, Boy Scout Troop 304, Breseman family, Paul Bullard, Don Cannon, Anna Coles, Ardis Dull, Len Englund, Brian Hart, Li-Chuan Huang, John Huth, Kent Jameson, Norm & Gerri King, Ken Lew, Bob & Suzy Lynch, Jim McNamara, Carl Moore, Debbie Newell, Michael Odom family, Sherrill Pattee, Craig Rose family, Mike Schuh, Roger Sharp, Robert Sunderland.

If you all don't come to meets, you'll probably not see your name in the newsletter until the next time you renew; also, membership cards are only available in person at a club function such as regular meet, meeting, pot-O-luck, but not at any ski-O meets, nor night-O meets, nor canoe-O meets (unless you are lucky), nor training events, nor workshops. If you want to get your handsome membership card, contact the membership coordinator, me (Harvey), at a Cascade sponsored meet or at one of the above-mentioned happenings. I do not have blank membership cards with me when I attend other clubs' functions. If you move, don't forget to turn in a change of address card; you don't want your newsletter to be late.

Now on to the fun stuff...Rick & Eileen Breseman's first child, Ryan, was born in January. Pat Albright and Peter Zurcher (Rocky Mountain O' Club) plan to marry in July and Pat will be moving to Colorado temporarily. If you want more news about people in the club, you'll have to tell me something or preferably write or scribble me something that is for this column since I have such a bad memory.
